payload.learning_objective: Understand the axioms that define a vector space (closure under addition and scalar multiplication, existence of zero vector and inverses) and why these abstract rules capture the essence of 'vectorness' through an axiom failure prediction game

mini_spec.title: Axiom Failure Prediction Game
mini_spec.narrative_wrapper: The student sees a strange candidate structure (like vectors with "max" as addition). Before testing each axiom, they drag a slider to predict how badly it will fail: from "works perfectly" to "completely breaks". When they click "reveal", the system shows a concrete counterexample with numeric values filling in, and their prediction marker stays visible next to the actual severity.
mini_spec.manipulated_variable: A prediction slider for each axiom showing expected failure severity (0-100%), which changes the student's committed hypothesis about whether and how much each axiom will be violated
mini_spec.primary_visual_feedback: As the slider moves, a colored bar fills from green through yellow to red, with text labels changing from "holds" through "slightly violated" to "totally broken", appearing directly on the slider track
mini_spec.causal_chain_exposed: After reveal, the concrete counterexample shows: the two specific vectors chosen, the operation applied step-by-step with intermediate notation, the left-hand-side calculation result, the right-hand-side calculation result, and the inequality or equality comparing them, all laid out vertically to show the logical flow
mini_spec.trace_strategy: Previous predictions remain as semi-transparent ghost sliders beneath each axiom's current state, with a small badge showing the prediction error magnitude, allowing students to calibrate their intuition across multiple structures
mini_spec.aha_moment: Vector spaces aren't just "things that obey rules"—different operations break axioms in dramatically different ways, and some non-standard operations (like componentwise maximum) feel almost right but fail specific axioms catastrophically while satisfying others perfectly
mini_spec.feasibility_sketch: Sliders, colored bars, text labels, and step-by-step counterexample display are all standard web components; no runtime risk beyond basic DOM rendering and simple arithmetic display logic.
mini_spec.weakness: The design assumes students will make *meaningful* predictions before reveal, but without scaffolding on what each axiom even *is* or why to care, many will slider-spam randomly rather than reason through them, reducing the calibration effect to noise.

payload.target_misconception: Thinking vector spaces are only R^n — not understanding the abstract axiomatic structure
payload.section_context: Second section after vectors, transitioning from concrete geometric vectors to abstract axiomatic structure
payload.design_brief: This is a lesson on Hilbert spaces for university students. The shared domain model is in src/data/model.ts with vector operations. This section introduces the 8 vector space axioms through interactive prediction. After this section, students will learn inner products, then completeness, then Hilbert spaces. Use soft colors (teal #62D0AD, indigo #8E90F5, amber #F7B23B for highlights). Include scaffolding text explaining each axiom before the prediction game.
